stem: A quality control inspector examines products from two assembly lines. Line A is responsible for 70% of total output and has a defect rate of 3%. Line B handles 30% of output, but its defect rate is uncertain — maintenance records suggest it lies between 5% and 9%. A randomly selected product is found to be defective. What is the probability it came from Line B?

ground_truth.answer: P(Line B | defective) ranges from 0.417 to 0.563 as Line B defect rate varies from 5% to 9%. Point estimate at 7%: 0.500.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(B|def) = P(def|B) * P(B) / [P(def|A)*P(A) + P(def|B)*P(B)].
The interval [0.417, 0.563] captures the range of posterior probabilities over the plausible defect rates.
common_failure_modes: Using only the midpoint defect rate and providing a single answer without uncertainty
Ignoring prior probabilities (the 70/30 output split) and comparing defect rates directly
Incorrectly assuming the defect rate is exactly known
